Title: Activation of alternative oxidase ensures carbon supply for ethylene and carotenoid biosynthesis during tomato fruit ripening

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Alternative oxidase family, mitochondrial terminal oxidase
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AOX1a activation is required at ripening onset to sustain carbon flux that promotes ethylene biosynthesis and timely carotenoid accumulation; aox1a knockout delays climacteric ripening and early carotenoid build-up.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR-Cas9 knockout (a1.1, a2.3); respiration and AOX capacity assays; western blot; RT-qPCR; GC-MS metabolite profiling; HPLC carotenoid profiling; oxygen isotope discrimination
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transcript increases during fruit ripening and peaks at red stage; functional AOX activity rises strongly at breaker stage
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AOX1a is the main AOX isoform functionally required during tomato fruit ripening. 2. aox1a knockout fruits show reduced AOX capacity, delayed onset of ripening, delayed ethylene-responsive gene induction, and delayed phytoene/lycopene accumulation. 3. AOX1a supports accumulation of aspartate and methionine for ethylene synthesis and pyruvate supply linked to carotenoid biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Natural on-vine fruit ripening across MG, BR, OR, R and time-course MG+ stages
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Slight delay in first fruit appearance; fewer fruits per plant; reduced fruit weight, height, and diameter
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although AOX1a transcript peaks late, in vivo AOX flux peaks at breaker stage, indicating substantial post-translational control by organic acids such as pyruvate and 2-oxoglutarate rather than transcript abundance alone.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Maintaining or enhancing AOX1a activity around breaker stage could improve ripening synchronization and support carotenoid accumulation, but constitutive loss causes yield and fruit-size penalties; stage-specific enhancement would be preferable.
